title: Unigram Language Model Segmenter
concept: SentencePiece uses a probabilistic unigram model to find the most likely subword segmentations, allowing soft tokenization during training.

exercises.prompt: What does SentencePiece use to find the most likely subword segmentations?

options.text: A dictionary of whole words only

exercises.explain: SentencePiece uses the Unigram model to perform soft tokenization by choosing subword splits based on probability.

cards.title: Cracking the Word Code
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how AI models split words they have never seen before? Instead of guessing randomly or using rigid rules, they use probability to find the most natural subword pieces!

cards.title: What is a Unigram Segmenter?
cards.body: A Unigram Segmenter is a tool that treats a sentence as a sequence of independent subwords. It uses probabilities to decide the best places to cut a text into smaller tokens. Instead of using strict rules, it chooses the combination of subwords that is mathematically the most likely to occur.

cards.title: The Chocolate Bar Slices
cards.body: Think of a sentence as a long chocolate bar with pre-made score lines. An ordinary segmenter only lets you snap the bar at those exact lines (like spaces between words). A Unigram Segmenter is like having a laser cutter: it looks at all the different ways you could slice the chocolate to get the most satisfying, clean pieces, even if they aren't where the standard lines are.

cards.title: Splitting "unhelpful"
cards.body:
Suppose we want to segment the word "unhelpful". A Unigram Segmenter might look at these candidates:
• "un" + "helpful"
• "un" + "help" + "ful"
• "u" + "n" + "helpful"

Based on how common these pieces are in its training data, it calculates that "un" + "help" + "ful" has the highest overall probability and chooses it!

cards.title: Myth: One Way to Split
cards.body: Many people think that text segmenters can only split words in one single, fixed way. In reality, SentencePiece's Unigram model uses 'soft tokenization' during training. This means it can segment the same word in different ways to help the AI learn to be more flexible and robust!

cards.title: Rule-Based vs. Probabilistic
cards.body: Let's compare rigid rule-based segmentation with probabilistic Unigram segmentation.

left.text: Splits text only at spaces. Fails or struggles when encountering new compound words or typos.

right.text: Calculates the most likely subword pieces (like 'sub' + 'word'), handling unknown words gracefully.

exercises.prompt: How does the Unigram model build its vocabulary compared to other subword tokenizers?

options.text: It starts with characters and merges them one by one.
It starts with a large vocabulary and removes the least useful subwords.
It only allows splitting at spaces.
It does not use a vocabulary at all.

exercises.explain: Unlike BPE (Byte Pair Encoding) which starts with individual characters and merges them, the Unigram model starts with a large vocabulary of subwords and progressively prunes (removes) the least useful ones.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The Unigram model allows for multiple different segmentations of the same sentence during training.
exercises.explain: Yes! By using probabilities, the Unigram model can output multiple different segmentations for the same text during training, which acts as a form of regularization.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how the Unigram model selects the best segmentation.
exercises.sentence: The Unigram model chooses the subword split that has the highest overall ________.
exercises.explain: The Unigram model evaluates different ways to split a word and selects the one with the highest overall probability based on training data.

exercises.prompt: Identify which system behaves like a probabilistic Unigram Segmenter.

options.text: System A: Splits strictly at spaces, leaving 'unbreakable' as one single giant token.
System B: Calculates probabilities to split 'unbreakable' into 'un', 'break', and 'able'.
System C: Splits every single letter individually regardless of meaning ('u', 'n', 'b', 'r', 'e'...).

exercises.explain: System B is the AI-assisted segmenter because it uses subword probabilities ('un' + 'break' + 'able') to segment the complex word, whereas System A uses rigid space-only rules and System C uses random character-level splitting.

exercises.prompt: How does a Unigram Language Model Segmenter choose the best way to split a sentence into subwords?

options.text: It randomly splits sentences without considering word frequencies.
It calculates the probabilities of different subword combinations and picks the most likely one.
It forces words to split only at punctuation marks.
It uses a dictionary of whole words only and never splits them.

exercises.explain: A Unigram Segmenter calculates the probability of each candidate token sequence. The segmentation that results in the highest overall probability (the product of individual token probabilities) is selected. This allows the model to find the most natural and frequent subword units.
